skill.name: flux-2-klein
skill.description: Use RunComfy to generate images with Flux 2 Klein, including fast 4B concepting, 9B polish runs, reference-image guidance, and model-specific prompt patterns.
skill.summary: Generate fast Flux 2 Klein images on RunComfy with model-specific prompt guidance.
skill.author: agentspace-so

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are Markdown examples or prose, not executable Ruby or weak cryptography. The confirmed risk is legitimate but meaningful: the skill directs agents to run the RunComfy CLI, submit prompts and tokens to a third-party API, and download remote generated files.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party CLI Execution and API Submission
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to install and run the RunComfy CLI, then submit user prompts to a RunComfy model endpoint with account credentials. This is expected for the skill, but users should consent before prompts, reference URLs, or tokens leave the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly documents CLI installation, CLI invocation, and POST requests to RunComfy with a bearer token. The behavior appears intentional and relevant, so this is a medium operational risk rather than confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: Remote Output Download to Local Filesystem
medium_findings.description: The skill describes polling RunComfy and downloading generated assets from whitelisted RunComfy hosts into a user-selected output directory. Host restrictions and a size cap reduce risk, but the workflow still writes remote content to disk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The download behavior is described explicitly, including a host allowlist and size cap. The mitigations are documented, but external file writes remain a real workflow risk.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Code Execution
low_findings.description: The many Ruby or shell backtick alerts are false positives. The flagged backticks are Markdown formatting around command examples, model names, fields, and prose, not executable Ruby code inside the skill package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is Markdown documentation and contains no Ruby source or executable backtick interpolation. The only real command risk comes from documented CLI commands, which is captured separately.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts do not correspond to cryptographic code. The flagged lines contain product description, numeric model guidance, prompt examples, and documentation text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No hashing, cipher, or password-storage implementation appears in the reviewed Markdown. The matches are semantic false positives caused by plain documentation content.

low_findings.title: Path, Scheduler, and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Benign Documentation
low_findings.description: The path traversal alerts point to abbreviated API paths such as request status and result routes. The at-command and reconnaissance alerts point to field tables, model-selection prose, and exit-code documentation, not scheduling or host inventory logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed context shows prose, API route descriptions, and token-storage documentation. I found no evidence of task scheduling, traversal logic, directory scanning, or system inventory collection.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Pattern: runcomfy CLI
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill relies on external `runcomfy` and `npx` commands. This is necessary for the workflow, but agents should verify installation source and user intent before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and central to the skill. The risk is dependency and execution exposure, not hidden malicious code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Pattern: RunComfy Model API
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow sends prompts and credentials to RunComfy endpoints and receives generated-output URLs. This should be disclosed because it moves user content outside the local workspace.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The outbound API host, bearer token use, polling, and result download flow are documented directly. The endpoints are first-party for the stated service.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Pattern: Token File and Output Directory Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents token storage under the user config directory and writes generated files to an output directory. Permissions and size limits reduce impact, but this remains filesystem access.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation clearly states local output paths and token storage behavior. The described 0600 token mode and download cap are mitigating controls.

content.value_statement: Fast image iteration often fails when prompts are vague or models are chosen poorly. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to use RunComfy Flux 2 Klein with clear prompt patterns.
actual_capabilities: Selects the 4B variant for fast concepting and live art-direction sessions.
Selects the 9B variant for more polished Flux 2 Klein outputs.
Builds subject-first prompts with scene, style, lighting, camera, and quality details.
Explains width, height, step count, prompt length, and aspect-ratio limits.
Supports guidance for up to four reference images through RunComfy.
Routes unsuitable requests toward stronger models for text, resolution, or detail.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI and a working RunComfy account or token.
Sends prompts and reference URLs to RunComfy over the network.
Does not self-host Flux 2 Klein or grant model access by itself.
Klein is weaker than specialized models for large text, 4K output, and extreme detail.

use_cases.title: Run live concept sessions
use_cases.description: Use the 4B variant to explore visual directions quickly during review meetings.

use_cases.title: Create product mockups
use_cases.description: Generate fast product visuals with controlled lighting, framing, and brand references.

use_cases.title: Prepare polished visual drafts
use_cases.description: Move from fast 4B concepts to 9B outputs with stronger detail and consistent prompt structure.

prompt_templates.title: Basic image request
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for a single image.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a Flux 2 Klein image of [subject] in [setting]. Use the fastest practical variant and save it to [folder].

prompt_templates.title: Product visualization
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate prompt for marketing or ecommerce assets.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a polished product image for [product]. Use Flux 2 Klein 9B, soft lighting, clear materials, and [aspect ratio].

prompt_templates.title: Reference-guided style match
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt using brand or style references.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use these reference images to match the visual style. Keep [elements], change [elements], and generate a Flux 2 Klein result.

prompt_templates.title: Model routing review
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for choosing Klein or another image model.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this image request for model fit. Use Flux 2 Klein only if speed matters more than resolution, text precision, or maximum detail.

output_examples.input: Generate a quick concept image for a ceramic mug on a wooden table.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects Flux 2 Klein 4B, writes a subject-first product prompt, and saves a fast concept image for review.

output_examples.input: Make a final square hero image for a cosmetics bottle with soft studio lighting.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects Flux 2 Klein 9B, uses about 25 steps, and emphasizes material, lighting, camera, and composition details.

output_examples.input: Use my brand references and keep the bottle shape while changing the label color.
output_examples.output: The assistant keeps composition and lighting stable, applies aligned references, and states exactly which elements should change.

best_practices: Ask for user consent before sending prompts, references, or tokens to RunComfy.
Use short subject-first prompts under the documented token limit.
Use 4B for exploration and 9B for final polish.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for private images without explicit approval.
Do not request 4K, extreme aspect ratios, or long embedded text from Klein.
Do not mix unrelated reference styles in one generation request.

faq.question: Does this skill run Flux 2 Klein locally?
faq.answer: No. It uses the RunComfy CLI and RunComfy hosted model endpoints.

faq.question: Which variant should I use first?
faq.answer: Use 4B for fast concepts. Use 9B when you need a more polished result.

faq.question: Can it use reference images?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents reference-image workflows for style transfer and guided composition.

faq.question: Is it safe for confidential prompts?
faq.answer: Use caution. Prompts and reference URLs are sent to RunComfy, so get approval first.

faq.question: Can it make accurate text in images?
faq.answer: It can try simple text, but GPT Image 2 is recommended for precise labels or multilingual text.

faq.question: What tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
